replies: Hi there! I'm ready to help you with cybersecurity questions.
Hello! Ask me anything about staying safe online.
Hey! How can I assist you with cybersecurity today?
followups: How can I create a strong password?

replies: Use a unique password for each account, with uppercase, lowercase, numbers and symbols. A password manager makes this painless.
A strong password should be long (12+ characters) and random. Store them in a reputable password manager.
Never reuse passwords across different sites — one breach exposes them all.
Passphrases (four random words) are easy to remember and very strong.
followups: Should I use a password manager?
How long should a password be?

replies: Phishing emails often look legitimate. Don't click links or download attachments from unknown senders.
If an email asks for credentials or money, verify by contacting the company directly — not via the email link.
Be cautious of urgent or threatening language — it's a classic phishing tactic.
Hover over links to see the real URL before clicking.
followups: How can I identify a phishing email?
What should I do if I clicked a phishing link?

replies: If an offer sounds too good to be true, it usually is. Take a moment, verify, and don't act under pressure.
Common scams use urgency, secrecy, and odd payment methods (gift cards, crypto). Walk away.
Report scams to your local consumer protection agency to help others.
Never share verification codes or one-time passwords — legitimate services never ask for them.
followups: How can I report a scam?
How do I get my money back?

replies: Share only what's needed. Review app permissions and revoke ones you don't use.
Use privacy-friendly browsers and search engines, and consider a tracker blocker.
Check your social media privacy settings — defaults are usually too open.
Be wary of free services; if you're not paying, your data often is the product.
followups: Which browser is best for privacy?
How do I review app permissions?

replies: Keep your OS and antivirus up to date. Don't run software from unknown sources.
Ransomware locks your files for a ransom — back up regularly so you never have to pay.
If you suspect infection, disconnect from the network and run a full scan.
Email attachments and pirated software are top malware delivery routes.
followups: How do I back up safely?

replies: Public Wi-Fi is risky — avoid logging into sensitive accounts without a VPN.
At home, change the router's default admin password and use WPA2 or WPA3.
Disable WPS on your router — it's a known weakness.
Consider a guest network for visitors and IoT devices.
followups: Do I need a VPN at home?
How do I secure my router?

replies: Two-factor authentication adds a critical layer of security beyond passwords.
Authenticator apps and hardware keys are safer than SMS codes.
Enable 2FA on every account that supports it — especially email and banking.
Save your backup codes somewhere safe in case you lose your device.
followups: What if I lose my 2FA device?

replies: Keep your browser and extensions updated, and avoid plain http sites for sensitive activity.
Use HTTPS, watch for the padlock, and verify domain names carefully.
Limit your extensions — each one is a potential attack surface.
Ad and script blockers reduce malicious ad and tracker exposure.
followups: How do I know if a website is safe?

replies: Updates patch security holes — install them quickly, especially OS and browser updates.
Enable automatic updates wherever you can.
Outdated software is one of the most common ways attackers get in.
Don't forget to update phones, routers, and IoT devices, not just laptops.
followups: How do I enable automatic updates?

replies: Social engineering tricks people, not computers. Slow down and verify requests, even from 'trusted' sources.
Watch for urgency, authority, secrecy — classic manipulation tactics.
If someone calls claiming to be IT or your bank, hang up and call back via the official number.
Train yourself to pause before clicking, replying, or paying.
followups: How do I verify a caller?
